title: Acute ST-Segment Elevation Myocardial Infarction (STEMI) -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
content: A door-to-balloon time of less than 90 minutes is the goal of a PCI-capable facility. This parameter is one of the core measures of the Joint Commission on Accreditation of Healthcare Organizations. [69] The door-to-balloon time is the time between the patient's arrival in the emergency department and the crossing of the culprit lesion by a guide wire in the cardiac catheterization laboratory. Teamwork between EMS, emergency department physicians, and interventional cardiologists sets the groundwork for optimal door-to-balloon times. The median door-to-balloon time in 2010 was 64 minutes, with 91% of patients receiving PCI in under 90 minutes and 70% in under 75 minutes. [70]

title: Myocardial Infarction -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
content: For patients who present with chest pain, the key to the management of MI is time to treatment. Thus, healthcare professionals, including nurses who work in the emergency department, must be familiar with the symptoms of MI and the importance of rapid triage. A cardiology consult should be made immediately to ensure that the patient gets treated within the time frame recommendations. Because MI can be associated with several serious complications, these patients are best managed in an ICU setting.

title: Anterior Myocardial Infarction -- Deterrence and Patient Education
content: Seeking emergency medical service as soon as possible is crucial for improving outcomes in anterior myocardial infarction. Therefore, upon arrival, obtaining an ECG is of the utmost importance, and guidelines recommend patients with suspected AMI should receive an ECG within 10 minutes of the first medical encounter.

title: Selecting a Treatment Modality in Acute Coronary Syndrome -- Function
content: Lipid-lowering agents are indicated in the treatment of STEMI. [12] [7] The choice has to be high-intensity statin, with careful risk and benefit assessment in populations with increased risk of side effects (patients with liver failure, elderly above age 75 years, previous side effects). The goal of treatment is a reduction in low-density lipoprotein (LDL) by more than 50% from baseline. Thus it is important to check lipid levels in patients with STEMI on admission. The timing of initiation of statin treatment has been investigated in the SECURE-PCI trial and showed a trend toward a beneficial effect of early initiation of statin treatment in a subset of patients with STEMI, but data demonstrates no strong statistical significance. [52] Given the absence of harm in administering statin therapy early, it is reasonable to administer a statin as soon as possible following the diagnosis of STEMI. Agents preferably used in this setting are atorvastatin or rosuvastatin. Based on data from the IMPROVE-IT trial, ezetimibe should be a consideration in patients with intolerance or contraindication to statin treatment. [53] Proprotein convertase subtilisin/kexin type 9 (PCSK9) inhibitors are a group of medications that lower LDL. The two largest trials evaluating this group of medications were FOURIER and ODYSSEY trials. Both trials demonstrated a reduction in major adverse cardiovascular events when adding these agents to statin therapy. [53] [54] The ODYSSEY trial involved patients who had ACS within 12 months of enrollment into the study. The major limitation of these agents remains cost. Regardless, they should be considered in patients on optimal statin therapy after STEMI, with the inability to achieve adequate reduction in LDL when assessed on outpatient follow-up.

title: Anteroseptal Myocardial Infarction -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
content: Anteroseptal MI is an electrocardiographic definition. The treatment of ASMI should be like any other acute coronary syndrome. An early invasive intervention strategy for patients with the acute coronary syndrome is usually the most acceptable approach in whom significant coronary vascular obstruction is a known entity. Low-risk patients with ASMI benefit substantially from guideline-directed optimal medical therapy, although this is often suboptimally used. The role of noninvasive imaging modalities is important in patients with acute coronary syndrome at intermediate risk and helps guide the choice of therapies: invasive versus optimal medical therapy.

title: Selecting a Treatment Modality in Acute Coronary Syndrome -- Function
content: Fibrinolysis should be a consideration if the patient first presents to a non-PCI capable facility and the anticipated time from first medical contact to the performance of primary PCI exceeds 120 minutes (this 120-minute duration usually accounts for a 30-minute door in-door out time (DIDO) time goal at the non-PCI capable facility). Upon deciding about whether or not to use fibrinolysis, it should preferably occur within the first 30 minutes of the first medical contact. This strategy can be achieved either as prehospital fibrinolysis by the emergency medical service (EMS) provider or within 30 minutes of arriving at a non-PCI capable facility (door to needle time). Contraindications to fibrinolysis require prompt review if this strategy is an option. Absolute contraindications are any previous intracranial hemorrhage, ischemic stroke in the past 3 months, central nervous system neoplasm or arteriovenous malformation, major head trauma or surgery within the past month, known active bleeding or bleeding diathesis (with the exception of menses), suspected aortic dissection, severe uncontrolled hypertension unresponsive to emergent medical therapy and use of streptokinase in the last 6 months. [7] Some relative contraindications include a history of chronic poorly controlled hypertension, surgery in the last 3 months, ischemic stroke more than 3 months ago, traumatic or prolonged cardiopulmonary resuscitation (CPR), and any other unspecified intracranial pathology. Some factors merit consideration when considering fibrinolysis based on the available evidence from the recent clinical studies. Mortality reduction in patients with STEMI who are undergoing fibrinolysis is the highest within the first 4 hours, being around 80% after 2 hours and less than 20% after 4 hours of symptom onset. [13] Patients with developed collateral circulation may have more significant benefits beyond the 2-hour timeframe when compared to patients without collateral circulation. The CAPTIM trial compared prehospital fibrinolysis with PCI in patients with STEMI and found that patients who received treatment with pre-hospital fibrinolysis within 2 hours of symptoms onset had better survival when compared to PCI strategy. [14] Based on the STREAM trial, prehospital fibrinolysis provides similar efficacy when compared to PCI in patients with STEMI who presented within 3 hours and were unable to get PCI within 1 hour but with a higher rate of intracranial bleeding. [15] [16] An overview of nine randomized trials published by the Fibrinolytic Therapy Trialist collaborative group suggests that characteristics of patients who derive the least benefit from fibrinolysis are those with a delayed presentation (over 13 to 24 hours of symptom onset), elderly (age greater than 75 years), inferior ST elevations, and uncontrolled hypertension (systolic blood pressure (BP) more than 175 mmHg). [17] Thus, as a general framework, it seems that patients who present with early onset of symptoms but with a significant anticipated delay in transportation to a PCI-capable facility should get fibrinolytic treatment in the absence of absolute contraindications. While on the other hand, patients who present with onset of symptoms longer than 12 hours and absence of hemodynamic instability and evidence of ongoing ischemia of a large amount of myocardium should not receive fibrinolysis due to a likely lack of significant benefit. Other cases require evaluation on a case-by-case basis with careful consideration of risks and benefits. Upon deciding for a fibrinolytic strategy, agents that are fibrin-specific and given as a bolus (such as tenecteplase and reteplase) are preferred to other agents due to demonstrated lower risk of intracerebral bleeding and are more convenient to use. [18] [19]

title: Selecting a Treatment Modality in Acute Coronary Syndrome -- Function
content: Antiplatelet agents have a well-established benefit in UA/NSTEMI patients. [61] [62] [63] After the diagnosis of UA/NSTEMI has been made or highly suspected, all patients should receive aspirin and subsequently continued on a daily maintenance dose. [21] [64] In addition to aspirin, all patients with UA/NSTEMI should receive therapy with a P2Y12 inhibitor. [65] [66] [11] The choice and timing of administration of the second antiplatelet agent will depend on whether early invasive strategy or ischemia-guided strategy was the choice and whether PCI, CABG, or medical management is the chosen option. PLATO and TRITON TIMI-38 trials have demonstrated more potent inhibition of platelets by ticagrelor and prasugrel when compared to clopidogrel, respectively. Thus for most patients, ticagrelor or prasugrel are the first choices of the second antiplatelet agents, but bearing in mind certain important caveats. In TRITON TIMI 38 trial, prasugrel has been shown to have less efficacy and increased risk of bleeding in patients who were over 75 years old, had a body weight less than 65 kg or had a history of a transient ischemic attack (TIA) or stroke. [23] These patients should not receive prasugrel. Also, patients who will not undergo PCI after coronary angiography should not receive prasugrel. [11] PRAGUE-18 trial demonstrated no difference in efficacy and safety between prasugrel and clopidogrel in patients with ACS undergoing PCI. [24] It is important to note that the PLATO trial included a subset of patients who were undergoing PCI, CABG, or treated medically. [22] Based on the results of the PLATO trial, it is recommended to use 81 mg aspirin dosing for long-term maintenance when using ticagrelor as the second P2Y12 agent. Higher dosing of aspirin (162 to 325 mg) may attenuate the efficacy of ticagrelor. Clopidogrel should be considered as initial therapy if there is a concern regarding cost and access to ticagrelor or prasugrel, or the patient is at increased risk of bleeding. In the subset of patients who are undergoing ischemia-guided strategy, the initial choice of second antiplatelet agent should be ticagrelor, with clopidogrel considered for the same reason as for patients undergoing early-invasive strategy. [22] Prasugrel should be avoided in this group of patients because the TRILOGY ACS trial demonstrated that prasugrel is associated with a slightly increased risk of bleeding when compared to clopidogrel in patients who are not undergoing primary PCI. [26] This data makes ticagrelor a safer option in patients undergoing an ischemia-guided approach when it is still unknown whether PCI will take place or not. Regardless of the initial strategy, a certain subset of patients with UA/NSTEMI will require CABG as a form of revascularization after defining coronary anatomy. These patients will, by that time, usually receive both aspirin and a P2Y12 inhibitor. The recommendation is that clopidogrel and ticagrelor should be held for five days before surgery, while prasugrel should be held seven days before the surgery. [11] [55] [67] Aspirin should not be interrupted for CABG. [11] [55] [67] Consideration to stop aspirin 3 to 5 days preoperatively is an option in patients with a significant risk of bleeding who are refusing blood transfusions. [11] Ultimately, unstable patients with UA/NSTEMI who have anatomy more suitable for CABG, have evidence of ongoing ischemia or hemodynamic instability should be considered for emergent CABG regardless of antiplatelet therapy.

title: Tenecteplase -- Administration -- Myocardial Infarction
content: The CAPTIM study demonstrated tenecteplase might be as effective as PCI in treating acute ST-elevation myocardial infarction (STEMI) when administered in the prehospital setting. [7] This strategy is desirable for patients who cannot receive PCI within the recommended 90 minutes after first medical contact. Indeed, up to 70% of STEMI patients present to hospitals without PCI capability, thus requiring transfer to a PCI facility or an alternative primary revascularization strategy. [8] This strategy is further supported by the American College of Cardiology/American Heart Association (ACC/AHA) guidelines, which encourage prehospital thrombolytic therapy within 30 minutes of symptom onset. [9]

title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 3 -- Community 2559
content: Acute Q wave infarction – widespread, acute ST‑segment elevation myocardial infarctions caused by occlusion of the distal left anterior descending coronary artery, the circumflex branch of the left coronary artery, the distal portion of the right coronary artery, the intermediate artery, the marginal branch of the right coronary artery, or the posterior descending branch of the right coronary artery, as well as acute ST‑segment elevation myocardial infarction involving the left anterior descending coronary artery and acute myocardial infarction due to left coronary artery occlusion, all represent distinct presentations of an acute myocardial infarction that share a common pathological substrate in the myocardium. Acute non‑Q wave infarction, acute non‑Q wave infarction – widespread, acute non‑ST‑segment elevation myocardial infarction (disorder), acute widespread myocardial infarction, postoperative acute myocardial infarction, and subsequent anterior non‑ST‑segment elevation myocardial infarction are additional variants of acute myocardial infarction, each reflecting different electrocardiographic patterns, timing, or clinical contexts, yet each also localizes its injury to the myocardial tissue. Collectively, these entities illustrate how diverse coronary artery occlusions, ECG morphologies, and clinical settings converge on myocardial damage as the central finding site, underscoring the importance of recognizing the specific type of infarction for appropriate diagnosis, risk stratification, and management in patient care.

title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 2 -- Community 1103
content: Acute myocardial infarction encompasses a spectrum of disorders that all involve injury to the myocardium. Specific forms include Acute Q‑wave infarction – widespread, Acute ST‑segment elevation myocardial infarction (STEMI), and Acute STEMI due to occlusion of distinct coronary branches such as the left coronary artery, the left anterior descending (LAD) artery, the circumflex branch of the left coronary artery, the diagonal branch of the LAD, the intermediate artery, the marginal branch of the right coronary artery, the posterior descending branch of the right coronary artery, and the septal branch of the LAD. Additional variants are Acute myocardial infarction due to left coronary artery occlusion, Acute myocardial infarction due to circumflex branch occlusion, Acute myocardial infarction due to right coronary artery occlusion, Acute myocardial infarction occurring during a procedure, and Acute widespread myocardial infarction. Despite the differing anatomic sites of arterial blockage and electrocardiographic patterns (Q‑wave versus ST‑segment elevation), each of these entities shares the common finding site of the myocardium, underscoring that the primary pathological substrate in all these conditions is myocardial tissue damage. Recognizing the specific coronary artery involved helps guide reperfusion strategies and risk stratification, while the universal involvement of the myocardium highlights the need for prompt diagnosis and treatment to preserve cardiac function.

title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 3 -- Community 1871
content: Acute infarct serves as a unifying morphological category that encompasses a wide spectrum of specific myocardial injury patterns. Within this category, Acute Q wave myocardial infarction is recognized as a distinct form of acute infarct. A broad range of Acute ST segment elevation myocardial infarctions (STEMI) are also classified under acute infarct, each defined by the precise coronary artery segment that is occluded. These include Acute ST segment elevation myocardial infarction due to distal left anterior descending coronary artery occlusion, due to mid left anterior descending coronary artery occlusion, due to occlusion of the circumflex branch of the left coronary artery, due to occlusion of the diagonal branch of the anterior descending branch of the left coronary artery, due to occlusion of the distal portion of the right coronary artery, due to occlusion of the intermediate artery, due to occlusion of the mid portion of the right coronary artery, due to occlusion of the posterior lateral branch of the right coronary artery, due to occlusion of the proximal portion of the right coronary artery, and Acute ST segment elevation myocardial infarction involving the left anterior descending coronary artery. In addition, Acute myocardial infarction due to occlusion of the circumflex branch of the left coronary artery is another specific morphology linked to acute infarct. More extensive presentations such as Acute widespread myocardial infarction and postoperative forms like Postoperative acute myocardial infarction are also captured within the acute infarct morphology. Together, these entities illustrate how acute infarct integrates diverse anatomic and clinical variants of myocardial infarction, aiding clinicians in precise diagnosis, risk stratification, and targeted management of patients with acute coronary occlusion.

title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 3 -- Community 2197
content: Acute infarct is a cardiac pathology that can manifest with a wide range of morphologic patterns. It is associated with acute Q wave infarction – including the widespread form – and with acute Q wave myocardial infarction. Various forms of acute ST segment elevation myocardial infarction are also linked to acute infarct, such as the general disorder and specific presentations caused by occlusion of distinct coronary segments: the anterior descending branch of the left coronary artery, the distal portion of the right coronary artery, the mid portion of the right coronary artery, the posterior descending branch of the right coronary artery, and right coronary artery occlusion overall. In addition, acute infarct encompasses acute myocardial infarction due to occlusion of the circumflex branch of the left coronary artery, acute myocardial infarction due to right coronary artery occlusion, and acute myocardial infarction that occurs during a medical procedure. Non‑Q wave morphologies are included as well, with both acute non‑Q wave infarction and its widespread variant being associated with acute infarct. Furthermore, acute infarct is linked to acute non‑ST segment elevation myocardial infarction and to subsequent anterior non‑ST segment elevation myocardial infarction, reflecting its relevance to both initial and later ischemic events. Together, these associations illustrate that acute infarct can present with diverse electrocardiographic and anatomic features, emphasizing the importance of recognizing its various morphologies for accurate diagnosis and management.

title: UMLS -- Run 34 -- Level 3 -- Community 2016
content: Acute infarct is a broad morphological category that encompasses a variety of specific myocardial injury patterns. Within this category, acute Q wave infarction – widespread and acute non‑Q wave infarction – widespread are recognized as distinct ECG‑based morphologies. Acute infarct also includes acute myocardial infarctions that arise from occlusion of particular coronary artery segments, such as acute ST‑segment elevation myocardial infarction due to distal, mid, or proximal left anterior descending coronary artery occlusion; occlusion of the diagonal branch of the anterior descending artery; occlusion of the circumflex branch of the left coronary artery; and occlusion of the right coronary artery at its proximal, mid, distal, or marginal branches. Additional associated morphologies are acute myocardial infarction due to left coronary artery occlusion, acute myocardial infarction due to right coronary artery occlusion, and acute myocardial infarction occurring during a procedural event. Together, these entities define the spectrum of acute infarct morphologies, linking the anatomical site of coronary obstruction with the resulting electrocardiographic and clinical presentation, which is essential for accurate diagnosis, risk stratification, and targeted therapeutic management.

content: Acute myocardial infarction can present in many distinct clinical and electrocardiographic forms, yet all of these entities share the common pathological involvement of the myocardium. The condition includes classic presentations such as Acute Q‑wave myocardial infarction and Acute non‑Q‑wave infarction – widespread, as well as various ST‑segment elevation myocardial infarctions (STEMI) that are defined by the specific coronary artery segment that is occluded. Examples of STEMI subtypes are Acute ST‑segment elevation myocardial infarction due to occlusion of the anterior descending branch of the left coronary artery, the circumflex branch of the left coronary artery, the proximal, distal, marginal, septal, or posterior descending branches of the right coronary artery, and the proximal left anterior descending coronary artery. Additional classifications encompass Acute ST‑segment elevation myocardial infarction involving the left anterior descending coronary artery, Acute myocardial infarction due to left coronary artery occlusion, Acute myocardial infarction due to occlusion of the circumflex branch of the left coronary artery, and Acute ST‑segment elevation myocardial infarction due to occlusion of the septal branch of the anterior descending branch of the left coronary artery. Non‑ST‑segment elevation presentations are also represented, such as Subsequent anterior non‑ST segment elevation myocardial infarction. Postoperative settings can give rise to Postoperative acute myocardial infarction. Despite the diversity of terminology, anatomical location, and timing, each of these acute coronary syndromes is characterized by injury to the myokard (myocardial tissue). Recognizing that the myocardium is the universal site of injury across all these variants is essential for accurate diagnosis, risk stratification, and the selection of appropriate therapeutic strategies in patient care.
